title: Ser vs Estar — two ways to say 'to be'
objectives: Use 'estar' for state and location (how/where someone is)
grammar_notes: SER = identity, profession, nationality, time. Permanent or defining traits: 'Soy rusa.' 'Es médico.'
ESTAR = temporary state, location, mood. How you feel or where you are: 'Estoy cansada.' 'Está en casa.'
Rule of thumb: SER = who you ARE, ESTAR = how you ARE.
Russian doesn't make this split — both are dropped or implied. Spanish forces you to choose.
example_phrases: Soy profesora pero hoy estoy cansada. (I'm a teacher [identity] but today I'm tired [state].)
Mi madre está en casa. (My mum is at home.)
